Title: Course Introduction & Tools
Topics: Introduction to Data Science - why data science - why now - course vision
Content:
Mini-lecture + live problem-solving: What is Data Science?

Additional Ideas to Cover:
- First Data Story Demo: Prepare simple dataset and walkthrough for rain analysis — Prepare interactive demonstration using 'Does it rain more on weekends?' as example question. Walk through all 6 steps: 1) Frame question 2) Collect data 3) Process/clean 4) Explore/analyze 5) Interpret results 6) Communicate findings. Use simple weather dataset that students can understand. Make it discussion-based: students suggest data sources, analysis approaches, potential confounding factors. Goal: show complete data science workflow without overwhelming technical details. — Interactive discussion, weekend rain example
- Course Introduction Prep: Slides for course intro, tools demo, 6-step DS process — Create slides covering: Why data science matters now, course philosophy (AI as learning partner), overview of 6-step data science process with simple example. Include tour of course tools: Blackboard Ultra (grades/announcements), Gradescope (submissions), Piazza (discussion), course website (langd0n.com with Notion board). Set tone for collaborative learning environment. Consider: opening poll about student interests, icebreaker activity, or data science in the news examples. — Course vision, tools tour, data science overview
- Programming Fundamentals Prep: Slides on variables, data types, grading structure — Create slides explaining programming basics for absolute beginners: What is programming (giving instructions to computers), variables as containers for information, data types (numbers, text, true/false) with real-world analogies. Cover course expectations: grading breakdown, accommodation process, academic integrity with AI tools. Include clear explanation of 4-5 hour weekly time commitment and how it's structured. Consider: cooking recipe analogy for programming, container metaphors for variables. — Programming concepts, course expectations, accommodations
- Programming Concepts Demo: Prepare class survey data for table creation exercise — Design hands-on activity using paper/pencil to demonstrate programming concepts. Create class survey (name, major, hometown, favorite color, etc.) and have students organize into table format by hand. Discuss different types of information and how to categorize them. Introduce concept of giving step-by-step instructions. Facilitate tech buddy partnerships for first few weeks. Include time for questions about course structure and expectations. — Paper-based programming practice, tech buddy assignment
- Tech Setup Lab Prep: Instructions for Jupyter troubleshooting, partnership facilitation — Prepare detailed instructions for TAs on: Jupyter server troubleshooting (common login issues, browser compatibility, password resets), facilitating tech buddy partnerships, basic programming concepts review. Include backup plans for students who can't access Jupyter (paper exercises, observation roles). Create simple programming exercises for students who get connected early. Provide scripts for explaining course tools (Gradescope submission, Piazza posting). Include checklist for ensuring all students have necessary accounts. — TA materials for tech setup and programming intro

Title: Grading Structure & Assessment Philosophy
Topics: How learning works in this course
Content:
Assessment philosophy + grading breakdown

Additional Ideas to Cover:
- Grading Structure Prep: Detailed slides on portfolio, demos, mini-exams, capstone — Create comprehensive presentation on course assessment philosophy: focus on understanding over memorization, multiple ways to demonstrate learning, growth-oriented approach. Detail grading breakdown: 30% GenAI Exploration Portfolio (completion-based), 25% Friday Understanding Demos (TA-verified), 25% Mini-Exams (3 total), 20% Capstone Project. Explain accommodation process clearly. Emphasize that AI collaboration is encouraged in exploration but prohibited in exams. Include examples of what 'understanding demonstration' looks like. — Assessment philosophy, grading breakdown, accommodations
- First Python Code Demo: Live coding examples, common error demonstrations — Prepare live coding demonstration in Jupyter showing: creating variables with different data types, basic operations, common beginner errors and how to debug them. Use examples that connect to real data (student_name = 'Alice', age = 20, gpa = 3.7). Show how Python displays different data types, what happens when you mix types inappropriately. Include deliberate mistakes to show debugging process. Have students follow along in paired work, with backup for those still having tech issues. — Variables and data types in Python
- Number Systems Prep: Interactive exercises for number conversion — Create interactive presentation on number systems: why computers use binary, how to count in binary (0-15), introduction to hexadecimal (color codes, memory addresses), brief mention of octal. Focus on conceptual understanding rather than memorization. Prepare hands-on exercises using calculators for conversion practice. Connect to real-world examples: color codes (#FF0000), file sizes, memory addresses. Introduce concept of lists as way to organize multiple pieces of information. — Binary, decimal, hex systems, lists introduction
- Number Systems Practice: Calculators for conversion, real-world hex examples — Hands-on practice session with number conversion using calculators and Python. Show Python functions for base conversion (bin(), hex(), int()). Create lists in Python to organize information. Use real examples: hex color codes in web design, binary in file sizes. Students work in pairs on conversion exercises and list creation. Include fun elements: binary counting on fingers, hex color picker exploration, creating lists of class data. — Number conversion, lists in Python
- Programming Foundations Lab Prep: Final tech resolution protocols, programming practice guidance — Prepare TA materials for final round of tech issue resolution, programming concept reinforcement exercises, mini-assessment protocols. Include exercises for students to practice: variable creation, data type identification, simple list operations. Create understanding check rubrics: can student explain variable concept, demonstrate creating different data types, describe when to use lists. Provide backup activities for any remaining students without Jupyter access. — TA materials for programming foundations

Title: Introduction to Tables and Data Organization
Topics: From messy information to clean tables
Content:
Building first data table

Additional Ideas to Cover:
- First DataFrame Demo: Class survey data, paper-to-code transition — Live demonstration of creating first DataFrame using class survey data from Week 1. Show transition from paper table to Python code step-by-step. Demonstrate basic DataFrame operations: .shape, .info(), .head(), .describe(). Have students follow along in paired work, creating their own simple DataFrames. Focus on connecting conceptual understanding (tables) to practical tool (pandas). Include common error troubleshooting and debugging strategies. — Creating DataFrames, basic operations
- Data Tables Prep: Examples from daily life, clean vs messy data concepts — Create presentation on data organization: why tables are fundamental to data science, rows as observations vs columns as variables, examples from daily life (gradebooks, shopping receipts, contact lists). Introduce concept of 'clean' vs 'messy' data with visual examples. Explain why pandas library exists (regular Python lists aren't sufficient for data analysis). Include interactive elements: students identify table structures in their daily life, discuss what makes data hard to work with. — Tables, rows/columns, pandas introduction
- Real Data Quality Prep: Examples of messy real-world data — Prepare presentation on reality of data work: real data is always messy, common problems (missing values, inconsistent formats, typos, wrong data types), why data cleaning takes 80% of data science time. Show before/after examples of messy vs clean datasets. Discuss ethical implications of data quality decisions. Include examples from news/research where data quality affected conclusions. Set expectations that this is normal and important skill. — Data quality, cleaning concepts
- Dataset Exploration Demo: Interesting but manageable dataset for exploration — Live exploration of real dataset with actual quality issues. Demonstrate loading data from CSV, initial exploration (.shape, .info(), .head()), identifying problems (missing values, wrong data types, outliers), basic cleaning operations. Choose dataset that's interesting but not overwhelming - maybe local weather data, movie ratings, or social media trends. Students follow along, discuss what they observe, suggest next steps for analysis. — Loading and exploring real datasets
- Data Tables Lab Prep: Understanding check protocols, hands-on practice datasets — Prepare materials for TA to verify student understanding of table concepts and DataFrame operations. Include datasets for hands-on practice, rubrics for understanding checks (can student explain row vs column, create simple DataFrame, identify data quality issues), troubleshooting guides for common pandas errors. Provide structured activities for students to practice DataFrame creation and exploration with different datasets. — TA materials for data tables practice

Title: Data Manipulation Fundamentals
Content:
Filtering - selecting - sorting

Additional Ideas to Cover:
- Data Manipulation Prep: Question-driven analysis examples — Prepare presentation on data manipulation as question-answering tool: filtering rows to focus on subsets, selecting columns for specific analysis, sorting to find patterns. Frame each operation as way to ask questions of data ('Show me only students with GPA > 3.5', 'What are the top 10 highest values?'). Include conceptual explanations before diving into pandas syntax. Emphasize building analytical thinking alongside technical skills. — Filtering, selecting, sorting concepts
- Data Manipulation Lab Prep: Challenge dataset, understanding check rubrics — Prepare challenge dataset and questions for students to answer using data manipulation techniques. Create rubrics for understanding checks: can student filter data appropriately, interpret group comparisons, explain their analytical approach. Include troubleshooting guide for common pandas errors (key errors, indexing issues, groupby problems). Design activities that require combining multiple operations to answer complex questions. — TA materials for manipulation practice
- Data Manipulation Lab Session: Question-answering with data manipulation techniques — Students work through challenge dataset answering specific questions using filtering, grouping, and summary statistics. TA provides individual support and verifies understanding through brief demonstrations. Focus on analytical process: breaking complex questions into manageable data operations. — Data manipulation challenge
- Data Questions Demo: Dataset with interesting patterns for filtering/sorting — Live demonstration using dataset with interesting patterns. Pose questions and show how to answer with data manipulation: filtering (.loc, boolean indexing), selecting columns, sorting (.sort_values()). Title: Introduction to Data Visualization
Content:
Creating first charts

Additional Ideas to Cover:
- Visualization Concepts Prep: Examples from news/media, chart selection guidelines — Prepare presentation on visualization principles: why we visualize data (pattern recognition, communication), different chart types serve different purposes, characteristics of effective vs misleading visualizations. Include examples from news, social media, research papers showing good and bad practices. Cover basic chart types: bar charts (categorical data), histograms (distributions), scatter plots (relationships), line plots (time series). Emphasize choosing right visualization for the question and data type. — Chart types, visualization purposes, good vs bad charts
- First Charts Demo: Simple datasets for each chart type demonstration — Live demonstration creating basic charts using matplotlib/pandas plotting. Start with bar chart for categorical data, move to histogram for distribution, create scatter plot for relationships. Students follow along creating their own versions. Focus on when to use each chart type and basic interpretation. Include customization basics: titles, axis labels, colors. Choose simple datasets where chart patterns are clear and meaningful. — Creating bar charts, histograms, scatter plots
- Chart Interpretation Prep: Chart analysis examples, concept review materials — Prepare materials for chart interpretation practice: what to look for in different chart types, how to describe patterns precisely, common misinterpretations to avoid. Include variety of charts for analysis practice. Review key concepts from Weeks 1-5 in preparation for mini-exam. Focus on building vocabulary for describing data patterns and chart characteristics. Include examples of misleading visualizations and how to spot them. — Reading charts, pattern recognition, assessment prep
- Assessment Preparation: Mini-exam preview, peer explanation activities — Review session covering programming fundamentals, data manipulation, and basic visualization. Students practice explaining concepts to each other without AI assistance. Provide mini-exam overview: format, content areas, what 'understanding demonstration' means. Include practice problems similar to exam format. Focus on building confidence in explaining their thinking process rather than just getting right answers. — Review of Weeks 1-5, explanation practice
- Mini-Exam #1: 35-minute assessment of foundational understanding — 35-minute exam testing foundational concepts without AI assistance. Mix of conceptual questions (explain variables/data types, describe when to use different charts), simple coding tasks (create DataFrame, filter data, make basic chart), and chart interpretation (describe patterns, identify chart type). Focus on understanding rather than memorization. Include accommodations for extended time. Design to verify students can work independently with core concepts. — Fundamental concepts, basic coding, chart interpretation
